summary: Muratori describes a 2007 lecture he gave at MIT's GAMBIT game lab about the importance of understanding programming and math at a deep level rather than treating tools as black boxes. Even before starting Handmade Hero, he was concerned about newer programmers lacking core low-level skills. He re-recorded the lecture in 2018 for his Handmade Hero stream, and notes that a discussion of normal map generation complexities would further illustrate why deep understanding matters.
key_points: Newer generations of programmers increasingly lack core, low-level programming skills
Understanding programming and math at a deep level is critical rather than just learning to use premade tools as abstract black boxes
The complexities behind normal map generation serve as another example of why deep technical understanding matters
Muratori's concerns about abstraction-dependent programming predated Handmade Hero by several years
money_quotes: I was already feeling troubled by the lack of core, low-level programming skills I was seeing in most newer generations of programmers.

I wanted to try giving a lecture that explained in more detail why it was so important for people to understand programming (and math) at a deep level, and not just learn to use premade things as abstract tools.
key_insight: Programmers who treat tools as opaque black boxes without understanding the underlying programming and math fundamentals are fundamentally limited in their ability to solve real problems.
